title: Ocular Hazards & Anatomy
description: How different laser wavelengths affect different parts of the eye — cornea, lens, and retina.

phases.title: The Eye: A Precise Instrument
phases.content: The human eye is an extraordinary optical system. Understanding its structure is crucial because different parts absorb different wavelengths, leading to different injury patterns and symptoms.

phases.title: Ocular Anatomy and Laser Effects
phases.content: Laser eye injuries depend critically on wavelength due to varying absorption in different ocular structures. The cornea absorbs UV and far-IR wavelengths, the lens absorbs UV-A, and the retina is most vulnerable to visible and near-IR light. The eyes optics focus visible and near-IR laser light onto the retina, concentrating the beam energy more than 100,000 times, which can cause permanent damage without any pain sensation.

phases.title: Wavelength Analysis
phases.content:
For each wavelength, identify the primary structure at risk:

1. 355nm (UV) - used in some laser shows
2. 532nm (green) - common DPSS laser
3. 808nm (near-IR) - diode laser pump source
4. 1064nm (near-IR) - Nd:YAG fundamental
5. 10.6μm (far-IR) - CO2 laser

keyTakeaways: The retina is the most vulnerable ocular structure for visible and near-IR wavelengths
UV lasers (200-400nm) primarily damage the cornea and lens
Far-IR lasers (>1400nm) are absorbed by water in the cornea and vitreous
Retinal damage is often painless and permanent
